# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`
**Distinguish between Quality Assurance (QA) and Quality Control (QC) in a shipbuilding context, giving one shipyard example of each.**

### Model Answer (3 marks allocation):
- **(1 mark)** **QA Definition:** Quality Assurance is the *systemic, proactive*, planned set of activities within the QMS (ISO 9001) that ensures processes are capable of producing conforming output. It is **process-oriented** and *prevents* defects.
- **(1 mark)** **QC Definition:** Quality Control is the *inspection-oriented, reactive* set of activities — measurement, NDT, dimensional surveys — used to *detect* whether output meets specification. It is **product-oriented**.
- **(1 mark)** **Shipyard Examples:**
  - **QA example:** Writing a **Welding Procedure Specification (WPS)** approved under **ISO 15614** and qualifying welders per **ISO 9606** *before* production begins.
  - **QC example:** Performing **Ultrasonic Testing (UT)** on a finished butt weld in the double bottom to verify absence of lack-of-fusion, per the **Inspection and Test Plan (ITP)**.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`
**Define the term "Process Capability Index (Cpk)" and state the minimum Cpk value generally accepted by classification societies for critical shipbuilding processes.**

### Model Answer (3 marks):
- **(1 mark)** **Definition:** $C_{pk}$ is a dimensionless statistical measure of a process's ability to produce output within specification limits, accounting for both **variability** ($\sigma$) and **centering** ($\mu$).
- **(1 mark)** **Formula:**
$$C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma},\; \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)$$
- **(1 mark)** **KTU Answer:** The minimum Cpk value generally accepted by IACS members for critical shipbuilding processes (e.g., panel-line welding, hull block dimensional control) is **$C_{pk} \geq 1.33$**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Explain the **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) cycle** as applied to shipbuilding Quality Management, with at least three shipyard-specific activities for each phase. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A panel-line welding process produces fillet welds with **USL = 4.5 mm, LSL = 3.0 mm**. From 25 subgroups of size $n = 5$, the grand mean is $\bar{\bar{X}} = 3.70$ mm and the average range is $\bar{R} = 0.40$ mm. Compute the **process standard deviation $\sigma$**, the **X-bar chart control limits**, the **Cp, Cpk** values, and comment on whether the process is acceptable. Use $d_2 = 2.326$ and $A_2 = 0.577$. **(7 marks)**

### Model Answer — Part (a) — 7 Marks

> **[2 marks — Concept]** The PDCA cycle is the four-stage iterative quality management framework mandated by **ISO 9001:2015 Clause 4.4**. In a shipyard, it provides the structural backbone for continual improvement.

> **[5 marks — Four phases with three shipyard examples each]**

| Phase | Shipyard-Specific Activities |
|---|---|
| **PLAN** | (i) Draft the yard's **Quality Manual** aligned with **ISO 9001**. (ii) Develop and qualify **WPS** (Welding Procedure Specifications) per **ISO 15614-1**. (iii) Define the **Inspection and Test Plan (ITP)** identifying hold-points for class surveyor attendance. |
| **DO** | (i) Execute work packages on the panel line and in assembly shops. (ii) Maintain **welder qualification records** (ISO 9606) and **NDT operator certificates** (ISO 9712). (iii) Calibrate instruments — torque wrenches, UT probes, total stations — at scheduled intervals. |
| **CHECK** | (i) Carry out **dimensional surveys** of blocks using **laser tracker / photogrammetry**. (ii) Perform **NDT** (VT, MT, UT, RT) per ITP. (iii) Issue **Non-Conformance Reports (NCR)** for any out-of-specification item. |
| **ACT** | (i) Conduct **Root Cause Analysis** using **5-Why** or **Ishikawa fishbone** on every NCR. (ii) Implement **Corrective and Preventive Actions (CAPA)** and close out in the CAPA register. (iii) Feed lessons-learned into the **design office** and update WPS / ITP / training modules. |

### Model Answer — Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Compute $\sigma$ (1 mark):**
$$\sigma = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2} = \frac{0.40}{2.326} = 0.1720 \;\text{mm}$$

**Step 2 — Compute X-bar chart limits (2 marks):**
$$\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R} = 3.70 + (0.577)(0.40) = 3.70 + 0.2308 = 3.9308 \;\text{mm}$$
$$\text{CL}_{\bar{X}} = 3.70 \;\text{mm}$$
$$\text{LCL}_{\bar{X}} = 3.70 - 0.2308 = 3.4692 \;\text{mm}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Cp (1 mark):**
$$C_p = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma} = \frac{4.5 - 3.0}{6 \times 0.1720} = \frac{1.5}{1.032} = 1.453$$

**Step 4 — Compute Cpk (2 marks):**
$$C_{pu} = \frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma} = \frac{4.5 - 3.70}{3 \times 0.1720} = \frac{0.80}{0.516} = 1.550$$
$$C_{pl} = \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} = \frac{3.70 - 3.0}{0.516} = \frac{0.70}{0.516} = 1.357$$
$$C_{pk} = \min(1.550,\; 1.357) = 1.357$$

**Step 5 — Decision (1 mark):**
$$C_{pk} = 1.357 \geq 1.33 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Process is CAPABLE — Acceptable for shipbuilding (just above threshold)}$$

> **Comment:** The process is borderline. Although $C_{pk}$ exceeds the 1.33 threshold, $C_{pl}$ (the LSL side) is the limiting factor. The mean should be **re-centered upward** by approximately **0.05 mm** to balance both sides and improve margin.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a)** Describe the **ISO 31000:2018 Risk Management Process**. With reference to a shipyard, identify and rank **four major project risks** using a **5 × 5 risk matrix** (Probability × Consequence). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With reference to **dimensional control in hull block fabrication**, explain: (i) the **RSS tolerance stack-up** principle, (ii) **backward compensation** and **pre-settlement** techniques, and (iii) the role of a **laser tracker** in modern closed-loop accuracy control. **(7 marks)**

### Model Answer — Part (a) — 7 Marks

> **[3 marks — ISO 31000 Process]** The ISO 31000:2018 framework consists of **six iterative steps**:
> 1. **Establish the Context** — define scope (e.g., newbuilding of a 300,000 DWT VLCC), criteria, and risk appetite.
> 2. **Risk Identification** — brainstorm, use FMEA, HAZOP, and historical yard data.
> 3. **Risk Analysis** — assign Probability (P) and Consequence (C) on a 1–5 scale.
> 4. **Risk Evaluation** — compute Risk Score = P × C and compare to criteria.
> 5. **Risk Treatment** — avoid, reduce, transfer (insurance), or accept.
> 6. **Monitoring & Review** — continuous tracking via the **risk register**, plus **Communication & Consultation** throughout.

> **[4 marks — Four Shipyard Risks on 5×5 Matrix]**

| # | Identified Risk | P | C | Score | Band | Treatment |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hull block out-of-tolerance at erection | 3 | 4 | **12** | High | SPC + laser tracker survey |
| 2 | Major weld defect requiring re-fabrication | 2 | 5 | **10** | High | WPS qualification, 100% UT |
| 3 | Schedule slippage on critical-path block | 4 | 3 | **12** | High | Buffer, parallel erection, EVM |
| 4 | Occupational fatality during erection | 1 | 5 | **5** | Medium | ISO 45001, PPE, safety training |

> **Heat-map band rule:** Score 1–4 = Low (Green); 5–9 = Medium (Yellow); 10–15 = High (Orange); 16–25 = Extreme (Red).

### Model Answer — Part (b) — 7 Marks

> **[2 marks — (i) RSS principle]** The **Root-Sum-Square** rule states that independent random tolerances combine as
$$\sigma_{\text{total}} = \sqrt{\sigma_1^{2} + \sigma_2^{2} + \cdots + \sigma_n^{2}}$$
> This is **far less pessimistic** than linear (worst-case) addition and represents the *statistically expected* dimensional uncertainty in a block built from many sub-components.

> **[2 marks — (ii) Backward compensation & Pre-settlement]**
> - **Backward compensation:** Plates are **pre-deformed** in the opposite direction to the expected welding shrinkage. For example, if a 12 m plate is expected to shorten by 8 mm after welding the attached stiffener, the plate is cut 4 mm *longer* and pre-cambered so the *as-welded* length matches design.
> - **Pre-settlement:** During hull erection, the midship region is intentionally built **25–40 mm higher (hog)** at the slipway stage. As the hull is launched and loaded, it sags by exactly this amount, ending in the design (slightly sagging) condition required by class.

> **[3 marks — (iii) Laser tracker role]** A laser tracker (e.g., **Leica AT960, API Radian Pro**) is a portable, laser-based coordinate measurement system offering $\pm 0.05$ mm/m accuracy over 80 m range. Its role in **closed-loop accuracy control**:
> - **Real-time survey** of as-built points on a block (corners, seams, penetrations).
> - **Comparison against CAD model** (offset data) — deviation displayed in colour-coded 3D.
> - **Feedback** to the fitters, who adjust fit-up before welding — closing the loop.
> - **Audit trail** — every point is logged, providing traceability to class surveyor and to the yard's QMS database.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on This Topic — Examiner Observations:**
> 1. **Conflating QA with QC (loses 1–2 marks):** Writing *"QA is checking the product"* — **WRONG.** QA is system / process; QC is product / inspection. Always state which is **proactive** and which is **reactive**.
> 2. **Forgetting the "min" in Cpk (loses 1–2 marks):** Many students compute only one of $C_{pu}$ or $C_{pl}$ and report it as $C_{pk}$. Examiners expect **both** computed and the **minimum** taken. Even if your final answer is correct, showing only one side suggests you don't understand the concept.
> 3. **Skipping the units (loses 0.5 mark):** Always state "**mm**" for dimensional tolerances, "**dimensionless**" for Cp/Cpk.
> 4. **No mention of ISO standards (loses 1–2 marks):** A KTU board examiner expects at least one ISO / IACS / class society reference (e.g., **ISO 9001, ISO 5817, IACS UR S21, ISO 9606**). Bare answers without standard references lose marks.
> 5. **Misapplying RSS linearly (loses 1 mark):** Writing $\sigma_{\text{total}} = \sigma_1 + \sigma_2 + \cdots$ — examiners will deduct. Always use the **quadrature (square-root-of-sum-of-squares)** formula.
> 6. **Confusing FMEA RPN with Risk Matrix P × C (loses 1 mark):** RPN includes **Detection (D)**; Risk Matrix typically uses only **P × C**. Don't mix the two.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2: Quality & Risk in Ship Production**

### Quality Control (QC)
- **Reactive**, **product-oriented**, uses **inspection, NDT, dimensional survey**.
- Tools: **7 QC Tools** (Fishbone, Pareto, Control Charts, Histogram, Check Sheet, Scatter, Stratification).
- Standards: **ISO 5817** (weld quality levels B, C, D), **ISO 17635** (NDT sequence).

### Quality Assurance (QA)
- **Proactive**, **process-oriented**, embedded in **QMS = ISO 9001:2015**.
- Backbone: **PDCA cycle** (Plan-Do-Check-Act).
- Prevents defects through **WPS, WPQR, welder qualification (ISO 9606)**, process audits.

### Accuracy Control
- Three pillars: **Dimensional + Distortion + Alignment**.
- Tolerance stack-up: **RSS formula** $\sigma_{\text{total}} = \sqrt{\sum \sigma_i^{2}}$.
- Compensation: **Backward compensation, pre-settlement, line-heating**.
- Modern yards use **laser tracker / photogrammetry** for closed-loop control.

### Statistical Process Control (SPC)
- **X-bar / R chart** — control limits: $\text{UCL} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}$, $\text{LCL} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}$.
- **$A_2$, $D_3$, $D_4$, $d_2$** — control chart constants by subgroup size.

### Process Capability
- $C_p = (\text{USL} - \text{LSL}) / (6\sigma)$ — centered process.
- $C_{pk} = \min[(\text{USL} - \mu)/(3\sigma),\, (\mu - \text{LSL})/(3\sigma)]$ — realistic.
- **Target for shipbuilding:** $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$; world-class $C_{pk} \geq 1.67$.

### Six Sigma
- **DPMO** = $D \times 10^6 / (N \times O)$.
- **Six-Sigma quality** = **3.4 DPMO** = world-class benchmark.
- Major yards like Hyundai HI target **4.5–5.0 Sigma**.

### Risk Management
- **ISO 31000:2018** — six-step process (Context → Identification → Analysis → Evaluation → Treatment → Monitoring).
- **Risk Matrix:** P × C on 5 × 5 scale; bands **Low / Medium / High / Extreme**.
- **FMEA:** RPN = S × O × D, each 1–10. **RPN ≥ 200 → mandatory mitigation**.
- Risks cover **Schedule, Cost, Quality, Safety, Environment, Contract**.

### Key Standards to Memorize
| Code | Scope |
|---|---|
| ISO 9001:2015 | QMS |
| ISO 45001:2018 | Occupational H&S |
| ISO 14001:2015 | Environmental management |
| ISO 31000:2018 | Risk management |
| ISO 5817 | Weld quality levels |
| ISO 9606 | Welder qualification |
| ISO 15614 | WPS qualification |
| ISO 9712 | NDT personnel qualification |
| IACS UR S21 | Class rules on hull tolerances |
| ASTM E11 | Control chart constants |

### One-Line Mantra for Last-Minute Revision
> **"QA prevents, QC detects; Accuracy keeps geometry true; Risk management keeps the project from sinking."**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Project monitoring: Build strategy, progress monitoring, PERT charts
topics.content:
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## Build Strategy in Ship Production **Build Strategy** in shipbuilding refers to the comprehensive master plan that defines **how, where, and in what sequence** the hull structure, machinery, and outfitting of a vessel will be fabricated, assembled, and integrated — from raw steel cutting to sea trials. It is essentially the **production DNA** of a shipyard, dictating the flow of material, labour, and information across all workshops and erection areas. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** > In the 2024 Scheme, *Build Strategy* is examined under the Course Outcome **CO2**: *"Apply modern ship production planning and monitoring techniques to real-world shipyard scenarios."* ### Key Definitions (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology) | Term | Formal Definition | |------|-------------------| | **Block** | A three-dimensional hull sub-assembly produced in the panel/assembly shop, typically weighing **20–500 tonnes**. | | **Erection** | The joining together of completed blocks on the berth, slipway, or in a graving dock to form the complete hull. | | **Pre-outfitting** | Installation of machinery, pipes, cables, and equipment **into a block before** it is erected. | | **Post-outfitting** | Outfitting work carried out **after** the block is welded into the hull. | | **Unit / Grand Block** | A very large pre-fabricated assembly (often a complete section of the ship) joined out of several blocks. | | **Pre-Erection Stage** | All work in workshops before block lifting onto the berth. | | **Erection Stage** | Joining of blocks to form the hull on the building dock/berth. | | **Post-Erection Stage** | Outfitting, painting, testing, and commissioning after hull closure. | --- ## Progress Monitoring **Progress Monitoring** is the **continuous, quantitative tracking** of physical work, cost, and time performance against the planned schedule. It uses control charts, S-curves, Earned Value analysis, and milestone tracking to detect **deviations early** so corrective action can be taken. > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy:** Think of build strategy as the *recipe* for baking a cake (what ingredients, in what order, at what oven temperature), and progress monitoring as the *thermometer and timer* that tells you — every five minutes — whether the cake is rising on schedule or burning. Without the thermometer, you'd only discover failure when you cut the cake open. --- ## PERT (Program Evaluation and Review Technique) **PERT** is a **probabilistic project management tool** developed by the U.S. Navy in **1958** for the Polaris missile programme. Unlike CPM (which uses a single deterministic duration), PERT recognises the inherent **uncertainty** in engineering activities and uses **three time estimates** for every task to compute expected durations, variance, and the probability of meeting deadlines. It is exceptionally well-suited to **shipbuilding**, where each block fabrication involves hundreds of uncertain variables (welder availability, weather, steel delivery, design changes). --- ## Master Plan vs. Build Strategy vs. Progress Monitoring ``` Project → Build Strategy (WHAT & HOW to build) → Master Schedule (WHEN) → Progress Monitoring (ARE WE ON TRACK?) → PERT/CPM (WHAT-IF analysis & control) ``` # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Build Strategy — The Six Pillars ### Pillar 1 — Zone / Sectional Construction The hull is divided longitudinally into **zones** (e.g., Zone A: forward, Zone B: amidships, Zone C: aft) and transversely into **sections**. Each zone is built as an independent grand block. This **parallelises** work and reduces the critical path. ### Pillar 2 — Block Construction Philosophy - **Block size** is dictated by: crane capacity, transport route dimensions, workshop bay size, and welding access. - Modern yards favour **larger blocks** (500–800 t) to **minimise on-berth work** (which is the most expensive). ### Pillar 3 — Outfitting Philosophy (the heart of modern strategy) | Strategy | Description | Yard Type | |----------|-------------|-----------| | **Pre-outfitting** | Maximum outfitting inside the block before erection | Modern (Korea, Japan) | | **Post-outfitting** | All outfitting after hull completion | Traditional (old European yards) | | **Integrated outfitting** | Combination of pre + post + unit outfitting | Contemporary yards | | **Plasma/line outfitting** | Outfitting along the production line as blocks move | Mass-production yards | ### Pillar 4 — Erection Method - **Slipway erection** (end-launch): Traditional; limited to smaller vessels. - **Graving dock erection**: Dry dock flooded after completion. - **Floating dock erection**: Hull built in a floating dock. - **Land-based construction with skid launch**: Large vessels built on jacks, lowered to water via skid system. - **Shore-to-shore transfer**: Block transported by heavy-lift vessels. ### Pillar 5 — Stage of Outfitting & Zone Outfitting Outfitting begins **before hull closure** in specific zones — e.g., the engine room block is pre-outfitted with the main engine on the shop floor. ### Pillar 6 — Modern Tools (KTU 2024 emphasis) - CAD/CAM nesting for plate cutting - Robotics & automation in welding - Digital twin & Industry 4.0 monitoring - ERP-integrated production planning --- ## 2.2 Progress Monitoring — The Control Loop The fundamental monitoring cycle is: ``` Plan → Execute → Measure → Compare → Analyse → Correct → Re-plan ``` ### Tools & Metrics | Tool | Purpose | KTU Use | |------|---------|---------| | **Gantt Chart** | Visual time schedule | High | | **S-Curve** | Cumulative % complete vs time | Very High | | **Earned Value (EV)** | Cost & schedule integration | High | | **Milestone Tracking** | Event-based control | Very High | | **Line of Balance (LOB)** | Repetitive flow production | Medium | | **Man-hour curves** | Labour productivity tracking | High | | **Material consumption curves** | Steel/cable/piping usage | High | ### Earned Value Management (EVM) — Core Indices ``` PV = Planned Value (BCWS — Budgeted Cost of Work Scheduled) EV = Earned Value (BCWP — Budgeted Cost of Work Performed) AC = Actual Cost (ACWP — Actual Cost of Work Performed) ``` Three critical indicators: - **Schedule Variance** = EV − PV (negative = behind schedule) - **Cost Variance** = EV − AC (negative = over budget) - **SPI** = EV / PV (Schedule Performance Index) - **CPI** = EV / AC (Cost Performance Index) --- ## 2.3 PERT — The Mathematical Engine ### The Three Time Estimates | Symbol | Name | Meaning | |--------|------|---------| | $a$ or $t_o$ | Optimistic time | Best-case scenario (no delays) | | $m$ or $t_m$ | Most likely time | Realistic expectation under normal conditions | | $b$ or $t_p$ | Pessimistic time | Worst case (delays, rework, weather) | ### Expected (Mean) Time — Beta Distribution Assumption $$ t_e \;=\; \frac{a + 4m + b}{6} $$ ### Variance of Each Activity $$ \sigma^{2} \;=\; \left(\frac{b - a}{6}\right)^{2} $$ ### Standard Deviation $$ \sigma \;=\; \frac{b - a}{6} $$ ### Project Variance (along critical path) $$ \sigma_{\text{project}}^{2} \;=\; \sum_{i \in CP} \sigma_i^{2} $$ ### Project Standard Deviation $$ \sigma_{\text{project}} \;=\; \sqrt{\sigma_{\text{project}}^{2}} $$ ### Probability of Completion by Scheduled Date $T_s$ $$ Z \;=\; \frac{T_s - T_E}{\sigma_{\text{project}}} $$ where $T_E$ = sum of expected times on the critical path. The probability $P(Z \le z)$ is then read from the **standard normal probability table**. --- ## KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | Concept | Formula | Notes | |---------|---------|-------| | Expected time | $t_e = \dfrac{a + 4m + b}{6}$ | Beta distribution weighted 1:4:1 | | Activity variance | $\sigma^2 = \left(\dfrac{b-a}{6}\right)^2$ | Range divided by 6, squared | | Earliest Event Time (EET) | $E_j = \max(E_i + t_{ij})$ | Forward pass | | Latest Event Time (LET) | $L_i = \min(L_j - t_{ij})$ | Backward pass | | Slack | $S_i = L_i - E_i$ | Zero slack → critical path | | Project expected time | $T_E = \sum t_e$ (on CP) | Sum of expected times | | Project variance | $\sigma_p^2 = \sum \sigma^2$ (on CP) | Sum of variances on CP | | Z-value | $Z = (T_s - T_E)/\sigma_p$ | Used with normal table | | SPI | $\text{SPI} = EV / PV$ | < 1 behind schedule | | CPI | $\text{CPI} = EV / AC$ | < 1 over budget | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Boundary Conditions to Memorise:** > - $a \le m \le b$ (optimistic ≤ most-likely ≤ pessimistic) > - For a perfectly known activity: $a = m = b$, then $\sigma = 0$ > - $Z > 0$ means project likely finishes *before* deadline > - $Z = 0$ → 50% probability of on-time completion --- ## Real-World Engineering Utility In shipbuilding, PERT is applied to: - New-building project tendering (to give realistic delivery dates to owners) - Engine-room installation planning (high uncertainty activity) - Subcontractor scheduling (delivery of main engine, propeller) - Risk-based contingency planning (e.g., probability of completing sea trials before contract date) - Major repair/refit projects # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the PERT Expected-Time Formula PERT models each activity duration as a **Beta distribution** (chosen because it accommodates skewed, bounded real-world times). For a Beta distribution with parameters $(\alpha, \beta)$, the mean is: $$ \mu \;=\; \frac{\alpha}{\alpha + \beta} $$ Empirically, the original PERT developers found that approximating a Beta distribution with **mode** $m$, **lower bound** $a$, and **upper bound** $b$ gives the mean: $$ \mu \;\approx\; \frac{a + 4m + b}{6} $$ ### Step-by-Step Justification $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1: Range of variation} &\quad R = b - a \\[4pt] \text{Step 2: Mode-centred weight (1:4:1)} &\quad \text{Beta approx. mode} = \frac{4m + a + b}{6} \\[4pt] \text{Step 3: Final weighted mean} &\quad t_e = \frac{a + 4m + b}{6} \end{aligned} $$ This is the **only** formula KTU examiners test — no integral calculus required. --- ## 3.2 Derivation of Activity Variance The Beta distribution's variance with empirical approximations: $$ \sigma^{2} \;=\; \left(\frac{b - a}{6}\right)^{2} $$ **Why divide by 6?** Under the empirical PERT rule, the *range* $b - a$ is assumed to span approximately **6 standard deviations** ($\pm 3\sigma$ on each side of the mean), so: $$ \sigma \;\approx\; \frac{b - a}{6} \;\Longrightarrow\; \sigma^{2} \;=\; \left(\frac{b - a}{6}\right)^{2} $$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation of Project Variance (Central Limit Theorem) Each $t_e$ is itself a random variable. The **sum** of independent random variables (the critical path) follows a distribution whose variance is the **sum of variances** — this is the **additivity property of variance**: $$ \begin{aligned} T_E &= \sum_{i \in CP} t_{e,i} \\[4pt] \sigma_{T_E}^{2} &= \sum_{i \in CP} \sigma_i^{2} \end{aligned} $$ This additivity holds **only along the critical path** because non-critical activities have slack that absorbs their variance. --- ## 3.4 Derivation of Z-Value (Probability of Completion) Standardising a normal variable $T$: $$ Z \;=\; \frac{T - \mu}{\sigma} $$ For the **project completion time**: $$ Z \;=\; \frac{T_s - T_E}{\sigma_p} $$ where - $T_s$ = scheduled deadline - $T_E$ = expected project duration (sum of $t_e$ on CP) - $\sigma_p$ = project standard deviation --- ## 3.5 Exhaustive Worked Numerical Example — Hull Block Erection Project > **Problem:** A shipyard is planning the construction of a hull section. The activities and their three time estimates (in days) are given below. Construct the PERT network, find the critical path, expected project duration, variance, and the probability of completing the project in **35 days**. | Activity | Predecessor | $a$ (days) | $m$ (days) | $b$ (days) | |----------|-------------|-----------|-----------|-----------| | A (Steel cutting) | — | 4 | 6 | 14 | | B (Plate bending) | A | 2 | 3 | 10 | | C (Sub-assembly welding) | A | 3 | 5 | 13 | | D (Grand block assembly) | B, C | 5 | 8 | 17 | | E (Pre-outfitting) | D | 4 | 7 | 16 | | F (Painting) | E | 1 | 2 | 9 | ### Step 1 — Compute $t_e$ and $\sigma^2$ for each activity $$ \begin{aligned} t_{e,A} &= \frac{4 + 4(6) + 14}{6} = \frac{42}{6} = 7.00 \text{ days} \\[4pt] \sigma_A^2 &= \left(\frac{14 - 4}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{10}{6}\right)^2 = 2.778 \\[6pt] t_{e,B} &= \frac{2 + 4(3) + 10}{6} = \frac{24}{6} = 4.00 \text{ days} \\[4pt] \sigma_B^2 &= \left(\frac{10 - 2}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{8}{6}\right)^2 = 1.778 \\[6pt] t_{e,C} &= \frac{3 + 4(5) + 13}{6} = \frac{36}{6} = 6.00 \text{ days} \\[4pt] \sigma_C^2 &= \left(\frac{13 - 3}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{10}{6}\right)^2 = 2.778 \\[6pt] t_{e,D} &= \frac{5 + 4(8) + 17}{6} = \frac{54}{6} = 9.00 \text{ days} \\[4pt] \sigma_D^2 &= \left(\frac{17 - 5}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{12}{6}\right)^2 = 4.000 \\[6pt] t_{e,E} &= \frac{4 + 4(7) + 16}{6} = \frac{48}{6} = 8.00 \text{ days} \\[4pt] \sigma_E^2 &= \left(\frac{16 - 4}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{12}{6}\right)^2 = 4.000 \\[6pt] t_{e,F} &= \frac{1 + 4(2) + 9}{6} = \frac{18}{6} = 3.00 \text{ days} \\[4pt] \sigma_F^2 &= \left(\frac{9 - 1}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{8}{6}\right)^2 = 1.778 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 2 — Forward Pass (Earliest Event Times) $$ \begin{aligned} E_1 &= 0 \\ E_2 &= E_1 + t_{e,A} = 0 + 7 = 7 \\ E_3 &= E_2 + t_{e,B} = 7 + 4 = 11 \\ E_4 &= E_2 + t_{e,C} = 7 + 6 = 13 \\ E_5 &= \max(E_3 + t_{e,D},\; E_4 + t_{e,D}) = \max(11+9,\; 13+9) = 22 \\ E_6 &= E_5 + t_{e,E} = 22 + 8 = 30 \\ E_7 &= E_6 + t_{e,F} = 30 + 3 = 33 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3 — Backward Pass (Latest Event Times) $$ \begin{aligned} L_7 &= 33 \\ L_6 &= L_7 - t_{e,F} = 33 - 3 = 30 \\ L_5 &= L_6 - t_{e,E} = 30 - 8 = 22 \\ L_4 &= L_5 - t_{e,D} = 22 - 9 = 13 \\ L_3 &= L_5 - t_{e,D} = 22 - 9 = 13 \\ L_2 &= \min(L_3 - t_{e,B},\; L_4 - t_{e,C}) = \min(13-4,\; 13-6) = \min(9, 7) = 7 \\ L_1 &= L_2 - t_{e,A} = 7 - 7 = 0 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4 — Slack Table | Activity | $E_i$ | $L_j$ | Slack | Critical? | |----------|-------|-------|-------|-----------| | A | 0–7 | 0–7 | **0** | ✔ | | B | 7–11 | 7–11 | **0** | ✔ | | C | 7–13 | 7–13 | **0** | ✔ | | D | 13–22 | 13–22 | **0** | ✔ | | E | 22–30 | 22–30 | **0** | ✔ | | F | 30–33 | 30–33 | **0** | ✔ | **Critical Path:** A → B → D → E → F (or A → C → D → E → F — both have same length in this example) ### Step 5 — Project Expected Duration & Variance $$ T_E = 7 + 4 + 9 + 8 + 3 = 31 \text{ days} $$ $$ \sigma_p^2 = \sigma_A^2 + \sigma_B^2 + \sigma_D^2 + \sigma_E^2 + \sigma_F^2 = 2.778 + 1.778 + 4.000 + 4.000 + 1.778 = 14.334 $$ $$ \sigma_p = \sqrt{14.334} = 3.786 \text{ days} $$ ### Step 6 — Probability of Completion in 35 Days $$ Z = \frac{T_s - T_E}{\sigma_p} = \frac{35 - 31}{3.786} = \frac{4}{3.786} = 1.056 $$ From standard normal table: $P(Z \le 1.056) \approx 0.8546$ > **Interpretation:** There is an **85.46% probability** of completing the project within 35 days. --- ## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Tool | Situation | Best Tool | Why | |-----------|-----------|-----| | New, uncertain ship project | **PERT** | Handles uncertainty with 3 estimates | | Repetitive, well-known work | **CPM** | Single deterministic time sufficient | | Progress vs time tracking | **S-curve** | Easy visual | | Cost & schedule combined | **EVM** | Detects cost overruns early | | Block flow in line production | **LOB** | Models repetitive blocks | | Visual schedule for management | **Gantt** | Easy communication | | Risk-based tendering | **PERT + Monte Carlo** | Gives probability of meeting deadline | # 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]** **Define build strategy in ship production. List any four modern build strategies adopted in shipyards.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Build strategy** is the master production plan defining the sequence, location, and method of fabricating, assembling, and outfitting a ship's hull and machinery, integrating all workshops and the building dock/berth into a coherent flow. > > **Four modern build strategies:** > 1. **Pre-outfitting strategy** – outfitting installed into blocks before erection (minimises on-berth work). > 2. **Modular / unit construction** – ship built as large pre-assembled units (e.g., complete engine room unit). > 3. **Grand block / zone construction** – hull divided longitudinally into zones for parallel production. > 4. **Integrated outfitting** – combination of pre, post, and zone outfitting for optimum cost-time balance. > > *(Award 1 mark for definition, 0.5 each for any two strategies — full 3 marks for four.)* --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]** **What is a PERT chart? Why are three time estimates used instead of one in PERT?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > A **PERT (Program Evaluation and Review Technique) chart** is a probabilistic network-based project management tool that uses nodes (events) and arrows (activities) to plan, schedule, and control complex engineering projects, especially where activity durations are uncertain. > > **Three estimates are used because:** > 1. Real shipbuilding activities involve **uncertainty** (weather, supply delays, rework). > 2. A single estimate hides this uncertainty — using three (optimistic $a$, most likely $m$, pessimistic $b$) captures the **range of probable durations**. > 3. The Beta-distribution-based expected time formula > > $$ > t_e = \frac{a + 4m + b}{6} > $$ > then gives a realistic expected duration with quantifiable variance, enabling **risk-based probability analysis** of meeting deadlines. > > *(1 mark definition, 2 marks for justification.)* --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) ### Question A — **[Build Strategy + PERT Numerical]** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]** A shipyard is constructing a passenger vessel. The following table gives the three time estimates (in weeks) for major activities. | Activity | Predecessor | $a$ | $m$ | $b$ | |----------|-------------|-----|-----|-----| | A (Hull design) | — | 4 | 6 | 8 | | B (Mould lofting) | A | 2 | 3 | 4 | | C (Steel cutting) | B | 5 | 7 | 15 | | D (Block fabrication) | C | 8 | 12 | 22 | | E (Erection on berth) | D | 6 | 9 | 18 | | F (Superstructure) | E | 4 | 5 | 12 | | G (Painting) | E | 2 | 3 | 10 | | H (Sea trials) | F, G | 3 | 4 | 5 | **Answer the following:** **(a)** Draw the PERT network, compute the expected time and variance for each activity. Identify the **critical path** and find the **expected project duration**. (7 marks) **(b)** Find the **variance and standard deviation** of the project along the critical path. If the contract delivery deadline is **45 weeks**, find the **probability** of completing the project on time using the standard normal distribution. (7 marks) --- #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Forward Pass & Critical Path** **Step 1: Compute $t_e$ and $\sigma^2$** $$ \begin{aligned} t_{e,A} &= \frac{4 + 4(6) + 8}{6} = 6.00, & \sigma_A^2 &= \left(\frac{8-4}{6}\right)^2 = 0.444 \\ t_{e,B} &= \frac{2 + 4(3) + 4}{6} = 3.00, & \sigma_B^2 &= \left(\frac{4-2}{6}\right)^2 = 0.111 \\ t_{e,C} &= \frac{5 + 4(7) + 15}{6} = 8.00, & \sigma_C^2 &= \left(\frac{15-5}{6}\right)^2 = 2.778 \\ t_{e,D} &= \frac{8 + 4(12) + 22}{6} = 13.00, & \sigma_D^2 &= \left(\frac{22-8}{6}\right)^2 = 5.444 \\ t_{e,E} &= \frac{6 + 4(9) + 18}{6} = 10.00, & \sigma_E^2 &= \left(\frac{18-6}{6}\right)^2 = 4.000 \\ t_{e,F} &= \frac{4 + 4(5) + 12}{6} = 6.00, & \sigma_F^2 &= \left(\frac{12-4}{6}\right)^2 = 1.778 \\ t_{e,G} &= \frac{2 + 4(3) + 10}{6} = 4.00, & \sigma_G^2 &= \left(\frac{10-2}{6}\right)^2 = 1.778 \\ t_{e,H} &= \frac{3 + 4(4) + 5}{6} = 4.00, & \sigma_H^2 &= \left(\frac{5-3}{6}\right)^2 = 0.111 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Forward pass** $$ \begin{aligned} E_1 &= 0 \\ E_2 &= E_1 + t_{e,A} = 0 + 6 = 6 \\ E_3 &= E_2 + t_{e,B} = 6 + 3 = 9 \\ E_4 &= E_3 + t_{e,C} = 9 + 8 = 17 \\ E_5 &= E_4 + t_{e,D} = 17 + 13 = 30 \\ E_6 &= E_5 + t_{e,E} = 30 + 10 = 40 \\ E_7 &= E_6 + t_{e,F} = 40 + 6 = 46 \\ E_8 &= E_6 + t_{e,G} = 40 + 4 = 44 \\ E_9 &= \max(E_7 + t_{e,H},\; E_8 + t_{e,H}) = \max(46+4,\; 44+4) = 50 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Backward pass** $$ \begin{aligned} L_9 &= 50 \\ L_8 &= L_9 - t_{e,H} = 50 - 4 = 46 \\ L_7 &= L_9 - t_{e,H} = 50 - 4 = 46 \\ L_6 &= \min(L_7 - t_{e,F},\; L_8 - t_{e,G}) = \min(46-6,\; 46-4) = \min(40, 42) = 40 \\ L_5 &= L_6 - t_{e,E} = 40 - 10 = 30 \\ L_4 &= L_5 - t_{e,D} = 30 - 13 = 17 \\ L_3 &= L_4 - t_{e,C} = 17 - 8 = 9 \\ L_2 &= L_3 - t_{e,B} = 9 - 3 = 6 \\ L_1 &= L_2 - t_{e,A} = 6 - 6 = 0 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Slack Table & Critical Path** | Activity | $E_i$ to $E_j$ | $L_i$ to $L_j$ | Slack | Critical? | |----------|----------------|----------------|-------|-----------| | A | 0 → 6 | 0 → 6 | 0 | ✔ | | B | 6 → 9 | 6 → 9 | 0 | ✔ | | C | 9 → 17 | 9 → 17 | 0 | ✔ | | D | 17 → 30 | 17 → 30 | 0 | ✔ | | E | 30 → 40 | 30 → 40 | 0 | ✔ | | F | 40 → 46 | 40 → 46 | 0 | ✔ | | H | 46 → 50 | 46 → 50 | 0 | ✔ | | G | 40 → 44 | 42 → 46 | 2 | ✘ | **Critical Path:** **A → B → C → D → E → F → H** **Expected Project Duration:** **$T_E = 50$ weeks** --- **Part (b) — Variance & Probability** **Project variance (along critical path):** $$ \sigma_p^2 = \sigma_A^2 + \sigma_B^2 + \sigma_C^2 + \sigma_D^2 + \sigma_E^2 + \sigma_F^2 + \sigma_H^2 $$ $$ \sigma_p^2 = 0.444 + 0.111 + 2.778 + 5.444 + 4.000 + 1.778 + 0.111 = 14.666 $$ **Project standard deviation:** $$ \sigma_p = \sqrt{14.666} = 3.83 \text{ weeks} $$ **Z-value for $T_s = 45$ weeks:** $$ Z = \frac{T_s - T_E}{\sigma_p} = \frac{45 - 50}{3.83} = \frac{-5}{3.83} = -1.305 $$ **From standard normal table:** $$ P(Z \le -1.305) = 1 - 0.9091 = 0.0957 \approx 9.6\% $$ **Conclusion:** Only a **9.6% probability** exists of completing the project within 45 weeks. The shipyard must either compress the schedule (crashing), add resources, or renegotiate the delivery date. Adding activity G to the critical path would extend it to 50 weeks as shown. --- ### Question B — **[Build Strategy + Progress Monitoring (Alternative Choice)]** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]** **(a)** Explain in detail the **modern build strategy** adopted in contemporary shipyards with reference to **block construction, pre-outfitting, and zone outfitting**. How does this strategy reduce overall project duration and cost compared to the traditional post-outfitting approach? (7 marks) **(b)** Describe the **Earned Value Management (EVM)** method for progress monitoring of a shipbuilding project. Given the following data at the end of month 8 of a 12-month project, calculate the **Schedule Variance (SV), Cost Variance (CV), Schedule Performance Index (SPI), and Cost Performance Index (CPI)**, and interpret the results. - Budget at Completion (BAC) = ₹ 2400 lakhs - Planned Value (PV) at month 8 = ₹ 1500 lakhs - Earned Value (EV) at month 8 = ₹ 1300 lakhs - Actual Cost (AC) at month 8 = ₹ 1400 lakhs (7 marks) --- #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Modern Build Strategy (7 marks)** The modern shipyard build strategy centres on **maximising parallel work** and **minimising expensive on-berth activities**. 1. **Block Construction (2 marks):** The hull is divided into numerous three-dimensional blocks (typically 20–800 t each). Each block is fabricated independently in covered workshops where environmental conditions are controlled. Welding is automated using robotic lines, increasing productivity by 30–50%. 2. **Pre-Outfitting (2 marks):** Before a block leaves the workshop, machinery, piping, ducting, cable trays, and equipment are installed inside the block while it is still on the shop floor at convenient working height. This eliminates the need for scaffolding and overhead work on the berth. 3. **Zone Outfitting (2 marks):** The hull is divided into zones (forward, amidships, aft, engine room). Each zone is outfit in parallel by dedicated teams. The engine room zone, being the most complex, is built as a complete "grand block" with the main engine pre-installed. 4. **Comparison with Traditional Post-Outfitting (1 mark):** In the traditional approach, all outfitting occurs after hull completion on the berth, requiring extensive scaffolding, working at heights, and serial work flow. Modern pre-outfitting **reduces overall project duration by 25–40%** and **cuts man-hours by 20–35%**. --- **Part (b) — Earned Value Analysis (7 marks)** **Step 1: Definitions (2 marks)** - **PV (BCWS)** = ₹ 1500 lakhs — value of work scheduled - **EV (BCWP)** = ₹ 1300 lakhs — value of work actually performed - **AC (ACWP)** = ₹ 1400 lakhs — actual cost incurred **Step 2: Compute Variances (2 marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{SV} &= EV - PV = 1300 - 1500 = -200 \text{ lakhs} \\ \text{CV} &= EV - AC = 1300 - 1400 = -100 \text{ lakhs} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Compute Indices (2 marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{SPI} &= \frac{EV}{PV} = \frac{1300}{1500} = 0.867 \\ \text{CPI} &= \frac{EV}{AC} = \frac{1300}{1400} = 0.929 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Interpretation (1 mark)** | Indicator | Value | Meaning | |-----------|-------|---------| | SV = −200 | Negative | **Behind schedule** | | CV = −100 | Negative | **Over budget** | | SPI = 0.867 | < 1 | Only 86.7% of scheduled work done | | CPI = 0.929 | < 1 | Spending ₹ 1 to get ₹ 0.93 of work | **Conclusion:** The project is **both behind schedule and over budget**. The yard manager should investigate bottlenecks (likely steel delivery or welding delays), consider crashing the critical path, and reallocate resources. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Don't add variances of non-critical activities** — only activities on the **critical path** contribute to $\sigma_p^2$. > 2. **Slack calculation errors:** Slack = $L_j - E_i$ for the same event — students often subtract the wrong way. > 3. **Sign of Z-value:** If $T_s < T_E$, $Z$ becomes negative — students forget to look up the **lower-tail** probability. > 4. **Three estimates must be in same units** (days OR weeks, not mixed). > 5. **Critical Path can change** during execution — PERT must be re-run regularly with updated estimates. > 6. **Earned Value interpretation:** Negative SV/CV means bad news; SPI/CPI **less than 1** = unfavourable. > 7. **Block weight vs. crane capacity** must be checked — students forget to mention this in build strategy answers. > 8. **PERT ≠ CPM:** PERT uses 3 estimates (probabilistic); CPM uses 1 estimate (deterministic). Examiners will deduct for confusion. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Quick-Reference Revision Checklist:** - ✅ **Build Strategy** = master plan for sequence of fabrication, erection, and outfitting. - ✅ **Modern strategy** emphasises **pre-outfitting, block construction, zone outfitting, parallel work**. - ✅ **Block size** constrained by **crane capacity, shop dimensions, transport route**. - ✅ **Progress monitoring tools:** Gantt chart, S-curve, Earned Value, Milestone tracking, LOB. - ✅ **EVM Indices:** SPI = EV/PV, CPI = EV/AC; both < 1 indicates problems. - ✅ **PERT** uses **three time estimates**: $a$, $m$, $b$. - ✅ **Expected time formula:** $t_e = \dfrac{a + 4m + b}{6}$ — must memorise. - ✅ **Activity variance:** $\sigma^2 = \left(\dfrac{b-a}{6}\right)^2$. - ✅ **Critical Path** = longest path through the network = zero slack activities. - ✅ **Project variance** = **sum of variances on critical path only** (not all activities). - ✅ **Probability of on-time completion:** Use $Z = (T_s - T_E)/\sigma_p$ with **normal distribution table**. - ✅ **Forward pass** → Earliest Event Times (EET). - ✅ **Backward pass** → Latest Event Times (LET). - ✅ **Slack** = LET − EET (zero → critical). - ✅ **Boundary conditions:** $a \le m \le b$, and $\sigma \ge 0$. - ✅ **CPM vs PERT:** CPM for known repetitive work; PERT for new/uncertain projects. - ✅ **Central Limit Theorem** justifies summing variances along the critical path.

topics.title: Cutting processes and tools: Mechanical cutting, thermal cutting, general description of the various machines, photoelectric and NC-control devices
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — CUTTING PROCESSES AND TOOLS IN SHIP PRODUCTION

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST631 - SHIP PRODUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cutting processes and tools: Mechanical cutting, thermal cutting, general description of the various machines, photoelectric and NC-control devices

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> **Cutting Process:** In the context of naval architecture and ship production, *cutting* is defined as the controlled separation of steel plates, sections, and rolled profiles into required geometric shapes and sizes, forming the foundational stage of **steel-stock preparation** before sub-assembly, panel assembly, and erection on the building berth/slipway.

The cutting operation transforms raw shipbuilding steel (grade **EH 36**, **AH 32**, **DH 40** etc., per IACS standards) into the *lofted* plates and stiffeners required by the ship specification. Cutting is broadly classified into:

- **Mechanical Cutting** — separation achieved by shearing action, abrasion, or chip-forming processes.
- **Thermal Cutting** — separation achieved by localized melting, oxidation, or vaporization using concentrated heat sources.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Exam Tip:** Whenever a question on cutting is asked, examiners expect you to **distinguish** mechanical vs thermal cutting on the basis of (i) **heat-affected zone (HAZ)**, (ii) **kerf width**, (iii) **plate thickness capability**, and (iv) **edge quality for subsequent welding**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a thick cardboard box that you need to cut into a specific shape.

- If you use **scissors or a saw**, you are physically tearing the fibers apart — no heat, sharp edge, limited to moderate thickness. This is **mechanical cutting**.
- If you instead use a **hot wire or a focused flame**, you melt/vaporize a thin line, and the molten material blows away — easy to cut very thick cardboard, but the edges are slightly burned and the cut line is wider. This is **thermal cutting**.

In a shipyard, a 30 mm thick steel plate is more easily separated by a **flame** (oxy-fuel) than by a saw, but for thin 4 mm plates or for a *secondary* member like a longitudinal stiffener, mechanical shearing is faster, cleaner, and cheaper.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (bolded for recall)

- **Cutting Speed (V):** typically **0.4 to 6.0 m/min** for oxy-fuel; **1.0 to 8.0 m/min** for plasma.
- **Kerf Width:** **1.0 to 4.0 mm** (mechanical saw), **2.0 to 6.0 mm** (oxy-fuel), **1.5 to 3.5 mm** (plasma).
- **Heat-Affected Zone (HAZ):** essentially **zero** for cold mechanical cutting; **0.5 to 4.0 mm** for plasma; **1.0 to 6.0 mm** for oxy-fuel.
- **Standard Oxygen Pressure (oxy-fuel):** **0.4 to 0.7 MPa** for cutting up to 100 mm.
- **Plate Thickness Range in Modern Shipyards:** **3 mm to 300 mm** (Hull plates) and **up to 600 mm** for special offshore structures.
- **Tonnage of Steel Cut per Modern Yard:** ~**100 to 150 tonnes/day** from a single CNC plate processing line.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 module specifically groups *photoelectric* and *NC-control* devices under "control devices" — meaning they are the **brains** that drive the cutting machine along the correct path. Mechanical and thermal processes are the **muscle**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Cutting Processes

The following is the **canonical KTU 2024 classification** of cutting processes used in ship production:

### A. Mechanical Cutting
| Sub-Process | Principle | Typical Use in Shipyard |
|---|---|---|
| **Shearing (Guillotine)** | Shear stress exceeds UTS | Plates up to ~25 mm |
| **Punching / Nibbling** | Punch pushes through hole-pattern | Small openings, scallops |
| **Circular Sawing** | Abrasive/HSS teeth remove chips | Plates, sections, pipes |
| **Band Sawing** | Continuous band with teeth | Rolled sections, bars |
| **Milling (Slot milling)** | Multi-tooth rotating cutter | Straight edges on heavy plate |
| **Routing** | High-speed rotating bit | Contour cutting of thin plate |
| **Planing / Shaping** | Linear reciprocating cutter | Straight edges, bevels |
| **Abrasive Water Jet (AWJ)** | High-pressure slurry erosion | Non-thermal contouring |

### B. Thermal Cutting (per ISO 9013)
| Sub-Process | Heat Source | Max Plate Thickness |
|---|---|---|
| **Oxy-Fuel Gas Cutting (OFC)** — Oxy-acetylene, Oxy-propane, Oxy-natural gas | Chemical oxidation + preheat flame | ~**300 mm** (with powder injection up to 2 m) |
| **Plasma Arc Cutting (PAC)** | Constricted electric arc (~25,000 K) | ~**50 mm** (quality), **150 mm** (severance) |
| **Laser Cutting (CO₂ / Fiber)** | Focused coherent photon beam | ~**25 mm** (CO₂), **40 mm** (fiber laser) |
| **Laser-Plasma Hybrid** | Combined heat sources | ~**100 mm** |
| **Arc Gouging / Air Carbon Arc (CAC-A)** | Carbon arc + compressed air blow | Groove back-gouging, weld removal |

## 2.2 Operational Step Logic

### A. Mechanical Shearing — Step Logic
1. Plate clamped between **fixed lower blade** and **movable upper blade**.
2. Hydraulic ram (pressure up to **20 MPa**) drives upper blade.
3. **Clearance** between blades = (0.05 to 0.10) × plate thickness — critical for clean cut.
4. Shear stress $\tau = F/A$ must exceed the material's ultimate shear strength.
5. Plastic deformation → crack initiation → crack propagation → separation.

> **Why it matters in ship production:** Shearing is the fastest, most economical method for **straight cuts** on thin plate, e.g., for cutting flat-bar stock used as **stiffener webs**.

### B. Oxy-Fuel Gas Cutting — Step Logic
1. **Preheat** plate to ignition temperature (~**870 °C for mild steel**, **~1,150 °C for stainless**).
2. **Oxygen jet** of high purity (>99.5%) is introduced through central orifice.
3. Iron oxidizes exothermically: $\text{Fe} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{FeO} + \Delta H$ (the heat sustains the reaction).
4. **Molten oxide (slag)** is blown out of the kerf by the kinetic energy of the O₂ jet.
5. Cutting proceeds; **kerf** (cut width) = nozzle bore + ~1 mm oxidation spread.

> **Why it matters:** OFC is the **workhorse of shipyards** because of low operating cost, ability to cut very thick plate, and suitability for bevel preparation (30°, 45°, K-bevel) for heavy welding.

### C. Plasma Arc Cutting — Step Logic
1. **Pilot arc** ionizes the gas (air, N₂, Ar-H₂) between electrode and nozzle.
2. **Transferred arc** jumps from electrode to workpiece.
3. Constricting nozzle (water-cooled copper) compresses arc → **temperature rises to ~25,000 K**.
4. Metal melts and is **blown away** by high-velocity plasma gas (Mach 2 to 3).
5. HAZ is narrow due to high energy density.

### D. Photoelectric Tracing — Step Logic
1. A **line drawing** (template, scale 1:1 or 1:5) is positioned on a tracing table.
2. A **photoelectric sensor head** (with lamp + photodiodes) is mounted on the **cutting head carriage**.
3. As the head moves, the **edge of the line** modulates the light falling on the photodiodes.
4. **Error signal** (difference in light between two halves of the photodiode) is fed to **servo amplifiers**.
5. Servo motors correct the lateral position of the carriage in **real time**.

> **Why it matters:** Photoelectric tracing was the **first step in shipyard automation** (pre-CNC era, 1960s-1980s) and is still used for legacy yards cutting from **1:1 wooden or paper templates**.

### E. NC/CNC Control Logic
1. The required part shape is described as a **part program** — a sequence of commands (G-code: G00, G01, G02; M-codes; tool offsets).
2. **NC controller** interprets the program, computes **interpolated path** (linear, circular, helical).
3. Commands issued to **servo drives** → **servo motors** (AC brushless, with optical encoders).
4. **Closed-loop feedback** from encoders corrects positional errors.
5. Auxiliary functions trigger **ignition, oxygen valve, plasma start, height control**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **exam-favorite** for the KTU 2024 14-mark questions on cutting.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Application | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_c = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000}$ | Cutting speed (rotational process: saw, drill) | m/min |
| 2 | $f_m = f_r \cdot N \cdot z$ | Table feed (milling cutter with $z$ teeth) | mm/min |
| 3 | $\text{MRR} = w \cdot d \cdot f_m$ | Material Removal Rate (w=width, d=depth) | mm³/min |
| 4 | $\text{Kerf} = d_0 + 2\delta$ | Kerf width: $d_0$ = nozzle bore, $\delta$ = oxidation spread | mm |
| 5 | $P = F_c \cdot V_c$ | Required power at the tool | W or kW |
| 6 | $\text{HI} = \dfrac{V \cdot I}{v}$ | Heat Input (welding/cutting) = VI/speed | J/mm |
| 7 | $P_{O_2} = K \cdot t$ | Oxygen pressure (K ≈ 0.05–0.1 MPa/mm) for OFC | MPa |
| 8 | $v_{cut} = \dfrac{K'}{t^n}$ | Empirical OFC speed (n ≈ 0.5–0.7) | mm/min |
| 9 | $F_{shear} = \tau_{uts} \cdot A$ | Force required for mechanical shearing | N |
| 10 | $t_{clearance} = (0.05 \text{ to } 0.10)\cdot t_{plate}$ | Optimum blade clearance | mm |

> **Notation guard:** When writing $\vert F \vert$ in any table row, use `\vert` so the markdown table does not break.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **OFC** is the **economic backbone** of every shipyard and structural fabrication facility in the world.
- **PAC** is favored for **stainless steel** (e.g., LNG cargo piping) and aluminum (superstructure).
- **Laser cutting** is rapidly expanding in **small and medium** shipyards for thin plate (~3–15 mm) due to extremely narrow kerf and high edge precision.
- **Photoelectric tracing** is used for *one-off* hulls and *rework* where generating a CNC program is uneconomical.
- **CNC cutting lines** are integrated with **Material Flow Management (MFM)** and **Nesting Software** to maximize plate utilization (often >90%).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Material Removal Rate (MRR) for Saw Cutting

Let the **circular saw** have diameter $D$ (mm) and rotate at $N$ revolutions per minute. Let the saw blade have $z$ teeth, each with a feed $f_r$ (mm/rev) per tooth.

**Step 1: Peripheral speed of blade**
The circumference traced per revolution is $\pi D$. Therefore the linear cutting speed is:
$$V_c = \pi D N \quad [\text{mm/min}]$$

**Step 2: Effective feed per revolution**
Since the saw has $z$ teeth and each tooth feeds $f_r$ per revolution:
$$f_{rev} = z \cdot f_r \quad [\text{mm/rev}]$$

**Step 3: Table feed (feed per minute)**
Multiplying the feed per revolution by the spindle speed:
$$f_m = f_{rev} \cdot N = z \cdot f_r \cdot N \quad [\text{mm/min}]$$

**Step 4: Cross-section of chip**
Each tooth removes a chip of approximate width $w$ (tooth width) and depth $d$ (engagement depth):
$$A_{chip} = w \cdot d \quad [\text{mm}^2]$$

**Step 5: Material Removal Rate**
The MRR is the volume of material removed per unit time:
$$\text{MRR} = A_{chip} \cdot f_m = w \cdot d \cdot z \cdot f_r \cdot N$$

Reducing using $V_c = \pi D N$:
$$\text{MRR} = \dfrac{w \cdot d \cdot z \cdot f_r \cdot V_c}{\pi D}$$

> **Engineering interpretation:** A 600 mm dia saw with 72 teeth running at 1500 rpm with $f_r = 0.1$ mm/rev cutting at 25 mm depth produces:
> $\text{MRR} = (5)(25)(72)(0.1)(1500) = 1{,}350{,}000$ mm³/min = **1.35 × 10⁶ mm³/min**, equivalent to **~0.085 m³/h**.

## 3.2 Derivation: Kerf Width in Oxy-Fuel Cutting

The kerf (cut width) in OFC depends on the **nozzle bore** and the **chemical reaction spread** beyond the bore.

Let the nozzle bore diameter be $d_0$, and let the chemical reaction spread laterally by an amount $\delta$ on either side.

**Step 1: Define kerf as sum of contributions**
$$\text{Kerf} = d_0 + 2\delta$$

**Step 2: Empirical correlation for $\delta$**
For mild steel, the lateral spread is approximately:
$$\delta \approx 0.5 + 0.01 \cdot t_{plate} \quad [\text{mm}]$$
where $t_{plate}$ is the plate thickness in mm.

**Step 3: Example calculation**
For a 25 mm plate cut with a 1.2 mm nozzle:
$$\delta = 0.5 + 0.01 \cdot 25 = 0.5 + 0.25 = 0.75 \text{ mm}$$
$$\text{Kerf} = 1.2 + 2(0.75) = 1.2 + 1.5 = 2.7 \text{ mm}$$

> **Exam insight:** This is why shipyard nesting software subtracts ~3 mm from the cut path to obtain the final plate dimension.

## 3.3 Derivation: Heat Input in Thermal Cutting

The **heat input (HI)** controls HAZ, distortion, and edge hardness. It is the energy delivered per unit length of cut.

**Step 1: Energy per unit length**
Energy per unit length = Power / Speed:
$$\text{HI} = \dfrac{Q}{v_{cut}} \quad [\text{J/mm}]$$

**Step 2: Substitute cutting power**
For OFC, total heat = preheat flame power + exothermic oxidation:
$$Q = \eta \cdot (\dot{m}_{fuel} \cdot \text{CV}_{fuel}) + \dot{m}_{O_2} \cdot \Delta H_{ox}$$

**Step 3: Simplify for plasma cutting**
In PAC, the dominant term is the **arc power**:
$$Q = V_{arc} \cdot I_{arc}$$

**Step 4: Final heat input expression**
$$\boxed{\;\text{HI} = \dfrac{V_{arc} \cdot I_{arc}}{v_{cut}} \quad [\text{J/mm}]\;}$$

**Numerical example:** Plasma cutting at $V = 150$ V, $I = 400$ A, $v = 2$ m/min:
$$\text{HI} = \dfrac{150 \times 400}{2 \times 1000/60} = \dfrac{60{,}000}{33.33} = 1800 \text{ J/mm}$$

> **Note:** KTU 2024 question papers often ask to compute HIZ (Heat Input Zone) or compare edge quality.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Cutting Process

The selection logic used in modern shipyards can be summarized as:

$$
\text{Process} = f\bigl(t_{plate},\ \text{accuracy},\ \text{HAZ limit},\ \text{cost/m},\ \text{edge bevel}\bigr)
$$

| Plate Thickness | Recommended Process | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| 3–12 mm | **Laser / Shear / Plasma** | Speed, edge quality, low HAZ |
| 12–50 mm | **Plasma / OFC** | Economic balance |
| 50–150 mm | **OFC** | Plasma loses quality; OFC still efficient |
| 150–300 mm | **OFC (heavy)** | Only economical process |
| Stainless / Al | **Plasma / Laser** | OFC cannot cut (oxide issues) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Modern CNC Plate Cutting Line

The following Mermaid block represents the **block-level functional architecture** of a CNC plate processing line — the dominant configuration in modern shipyards.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[CAD Nesting Software] --> A2[Nested Plate Layout]
    A2 --> A3[NC Part Program G-code]
    A3 --> A4[CNC Controller Unit]

    A4 --> A5[Servo Drive X-axis]
    A4 --> A6[Servo Drive Y-axis]
    A4 --> A7[Servo Drive Z-axis Height Control]

    A5 --> A8[Gantry / Bridge Carriage]
    A6 --> A8
    A7 --> A8

    A8 --> A9[Cutting Torch OFC or PAC]
    A8 --> A10[Photoelectric Sensor Head]

    A10 --> A11[Analog Error Signal]
    A11 --> A12[Servo Amplifier]
    A12 --> A5
    A12 --> A6

    A13[Plate Stock] --> A14[Loading Conveyor]
    A14 --> A15[Cutter Bed]
    A15 --> A8

    A9 --> A16[Slag and Fume Extraction]
    A16 --> A17[Ventilation and Filter]

    classDef ctrl fill:#ffe5b4,stroke:#d2691e,color:#000
    classDef motion fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000
    classDef support fill:#f0e1f5,stroke:#6f42c1,color:#000

    class A1,A2,A3,A4,A11,A12 ctrl
    class A5,A6,A7,A8 motion
    class A9,A13,A14,A15 process
    class A10,A16,A17 support
```

## 4.2 Decision Tree — Selection of Cutting Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start: Identify Plate Spec] --> S1{Plate thickness t?}
    S1 -->|t less than 12 mm| S2{Accuracy demand?}
    S1 -->|12 to 50 mm| S3{Material type?}
    S1 -->|t greater than 50 mm| S4[OFC Oxy-Fuel Cutting]
    S2 -->|High| S5[Laser Cutting]
    S2 -->|Moderate| S6[Plasma or Shear]
    S3 -->|Mild Steel| S7[Plasma or OFC]
    S3 -->|Stainless or Al| S8[Plasma or Laser]

    S5 --> End[End of Selection]
    S6 --> End
    S4 --> End
    S7 --> End
    S8 --> End
```

## 4.3 Schematic of a Photoelectric Tracing Head

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Light Source Lamp] -->|Illuminates| TP[Template Line Edge on Table]
    TP -->|Reflects| L2[Photodiode Left Half]
    TP -->|Reflects| L3[Photodiode Right Half]
    L2 --> C1[Difference Amplifier]
    L3 --> C1
    C1 -->|Error signal| SV[Servo Amplifier]
    SV -->|Steers| CH[Cutting Head Carriage]
    CH -->|Maintains| TP
```

> **Note:** When the head drifts *off* the line, one photodiode sees more light than the other, producing a non-zero error voltage that drives the servo to correct.

## 4.4 NC/CNC Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PGM[Part Program G-code] --> CTRL[CNC Controller Interpolator]
    CTRL --> REF[Reference Position]
    REF --> ERR[Error Comparator]
    FBK[Encoder Feedback] --> ERR
    ERR --> AMP[Servo Amplifier]
    AMP --> MTR[Servo Motor]
    MTR --> TBL[Machine Table]
    TBL --> FBK
    TBL --> CUT[Cutting Torch]
```

> **Engineering insight:** The **closed loop** is what makes CNC accurate to ±0.1 mm over a 20 m span — essential for accurate hull lines.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**"Distinguish between mechanical cutting and thermal cutting processes used in ship production. Mention any two advantages and two limitations of each."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Mechanical Cutting | Thermal Cutting |
|---|---|---|
| **Principle** | Shear/abrasion removes material as chips | Localized melting/oxidation removes material |
| **Heat input** | Negligible — no HAZ | Significant — HAZ up to 6 mm |
| **Edge quality** | Clean, may need deburring | Slag + oxide scale, requires grinding |
| **Plate thickness** | Up to ~50 mm (cost-effective below 25 mm) | Up to ~300 mm (and beyond) |
| **Speed** | High for thin plate | High for thick plate |
| **Cost** | Low for thin plate, high for thick | Low for thick plate |

- *Mechanical* advantages: no HAZ, lower distortion, suitable for stainless/aluminum.
- *Mechanical* limitations: limited thickness, tool wear, high force.
- *Thermal* advantages: cuts very thick plate, beveling in single pass, low force.
- *Thermal* limitations: HAZ, slag, distortion, not for non-ferrous metals.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"Explain the working principle of a photoelectric tracing device used in shipyard plate cutting machines."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A photoelectric tracing device uses a **light source** and **two photodiodes** mounted on the cutting head. A **template** (1:1 or 1:5 scale drawing of the part) is placed on the tracing table. As the head moves, the light reflected from the white background and the black line differs. The two photodiodes receive different light intensities, producing a **differential error signal**. This signal is amplified and fed to **servo motors** that steer the cutting head carriage so that it follows the line accurately. The cutting torch (OFC or plasma) attached to the head cuts the plate in synchronism. The system provides tracking accuracy of about ±0.5 mm and was widely used in pre-CNC shipyards.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **Internal Choice Format** — answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Describe the **oxy-fuel gas cutting process** in detail, with a neat sketch of the cutting torch. Explain the role of (i) preheat flame, (ii) cutting oxygen, and (iii) cutting speed on edge quality. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the working of an **NC-controlled plate cutting machine** with a block diagram. Compare **NC control** with **photoelectric tracing** in terms of accuracy, flexibility, and productivity. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q A (a)

**Definition (1 mark):** Oxy-fuel gas cutting is a thermal cutting process in which the metal is preheated to its ignition temperature and then oxidized by a stream of high-purity oxygen; the exothermic oxidation sustains the cut.

**Apparatus (2 marks):**
- Oxygen cylinder (high purity, >99.5%) with pressure regulator (0.4–0.7 MPa).
- Fuel gas cylinder (acetylene / propane / methane).
- Cutting torch with preheat orifices + central cutting-oxygen orifice.
- Nozzle (bore 0.8 to 3.5 mm depending on thickness).

**Sketch (2 marks):** *[Refer to schematic in lecture — torch with concentric fuel + oxygen ports, oxygen blowing molten oxide from the kerf]*

**Role of parameters (2 marks):**
- **Preheat flame:** raises plate temperature to ~870 °C; insufficient preheat → incomplete ignition.
- **Cutting oxygen:** sustains oxidation and blows slag out; too low → slow cut, slag; too high → wide kerf.
- **Cutting speed:** optimum speed → smooth, vertical cut; too slow → wide HAZ, gouged edge; too fast → uncut slugs (drag lines).

**Conclusion (mark 7):** OFC is economical for plate >25 mm and is the standard process for ship hull plate preparation.

---

#### Model Solution — Q A (b)

**Block diagram (3 marks):** *(refer to Section 4.4)*
- Part program → CNC controller → servo amplifiers → servo motors → machine table → encoder feedback.
- Auxiliary I/O: torch ignition, gas valves, height control, fume extraction.

**NC control features (2 marks):**
- Path interpolation (linear G01, circular G02/G03).
- Multi-axis (X, Y, Z + bevel torch rotation A, B).
- Tool offsets, scaling, mirroring, nesting.

**Comparison table (2 marks):**

| Feature | Photoelectric Tracing | NC Control |
|---|---|---|
| Template | 1:1 drawing required | Digital part program |
| Accuracy | ±0.5 mm | ±0.1 mm or better |
| Flexibility | Low — new template for new part | Very high — change program |
| Productivity | Moderate | High (24×7) |
| Cost | Low initial | Higher but amortized |
| Data storage | Physical templates | Electronic library |

**Conclusion (mark 7):** Modern shipyards use **CNC** with **photoelectric tracing kept as a backup** for legacy templates and rework.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a **neat sketch**, describe the working of a **plasma arc cutting (PAC) system**. State any **four advantages** of PAC over oxy-fuel cutting. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is **kerf** in cutting? Derive the empirical expression for kerf width in OFC. A **25 mm thick mild steel plate** is to be cut using a **1.5 mm bore nozzle**. Calculate the expected kerf width and the **oxygen pressure** required (assume K = 0.06 MPa/mm). **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q B (a)

**Principle (1.5 marks):** A constricted electric arc (transferred arc) ionizes a gas (air / N₂ / Ar-H₂) into plasma at ~25,000 K. The high-velocity plasma jet melts the metal and blows away the molten material.

**Sketch (2 marks):** *(Mermaid/hand sketch — electrode, constricting nozzle, pilot arc, transferred arc, plasma jet, workpiece, water cooling circuit.)*

**Working steps (1.5 marks):**
1. Pilot arc strikes between electrode and nozzle.
2. Arc transfers to workpiece.
3. Plasma gas flow constricts the arc.
4. Molten metal is ejected.

**Four advantages over OFC (2 marks):**
1. Cuts stainless steel and aluminum.
2. Narrower HAZ and kerf.
3. Higher speed for plate 6–30 mm.
4. No preheat time; no flammable gas storage.

**Conclusion (mark 7):** PAC is the **preferred process for thin-to-medium plate**, especially non-ferrous.

---

#### Model Solution — Q B (b)

**Kerf definition (1 mark):** Kerf is the width of the cut (gap) produced by the cutting process.

**Derivation (2 marks):**
The kerf consists of the nozzle bore plus the lateral spread of the oxidation reaction:
$$\text{Kerf} = d_0 + 2\delta$$
$$\delta = 0.5 + 0.01 \cdot t_{plate} \quad [\text{mm}]$$
$$\text{Kerf} = d_0 + 1.0 + 0.02 \cdot t_{plate}$$

**Calculation (3 marks):**
Given $t_{plate} = 25$ mm, $d_0 = 1.5$ mm:
$$\delta = 0.5 + 0.01 \cdot 25 = 0.5 + 0.25 = 0.75 \text{ mm}$$
$$\text{Kerf} = 1.5 + 2(0.75) = 1.5 + 1.5 = \mathbf{3.0 \text{ mm}}$$

**Oxygen pressure (1 mark):**
$$P_{O_2} = K \cdot t_{plate} = 0.06 \cdot 25 = \mathbf{1.5 \text{ MPa}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Many students **forget the factor of 2** for lateral spread — you lose 1 mark.
> 2. Do **not** confuse the **blade clearance** formula ($0.05 t$) with the **kerf** formula — different processes.
> 3. When asked for **oxygen pressure**, state it in **MPa** (not bar or psi) — KTU 2024 uses SI units.
> 4. In PAC questions, do **not** claim plasma can cut "any metal" — it cannot cut thick plate with high edge quality.
> 5. Sketches must be **labelled** (nozzle, electrode, workpiece, arc) to get full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-minute KTU 2024 revision list — print and memorize:**

- ✅ **Mechanical cutting** = no heat, used for thin plate, secondary members, non-ferrous metals.
- ✅ **Thermal cutting** = heat-driven, used for thick hull plate (OFC) and stainless/aluminum (Plasma/Laser).
- ✅ **OFC cannot cut** stainless steel, cast iron, copper, or aluminum — the oxide melts at higher temperature than the base metal.
- ✅ **Plasma arc temperature ≈ 25,000 K**; oxy-fuel preheat ≈ **3,100 K**; laser focal spot ≈ **10,000 K**.
- ✅ **Kerf width** increases with **plate thickness** and **oxygen purity**.
- ✅ **Clearance** for shearing blades = **5–10 % of plate thickness** (too tight → burrs; too loose → jagged).
- ✅ **Heat Input (HI)** = (V × I)/v; lower HI = less HAZ and distortion.
- ✅ **Photoelectric tracing** uses a 1:1 (or 1:5) **template**, two photodiodes, and a **differential error signal** to drive servos.
- ✅ **NC control** interprets **G-codes** (linear/circular interpolation), **M-codes** (auxiliary), and uses **closed-loop servo** with encoders.
- ✅ **CNC machines** integrate with **CAD nesting** software to optimize plate utilization (often >90%).
- ✅ **Gantry-type** plate cutters are most common in shipyards; **cantilever** and **bridge** types are also used.
- ✅ **OFC speed** varies inversely with **thickness** — typical 250–500 mm/min for 25 mm plate.
- ✅ **Plasma quality cut** up to ~**50 mm**; **severance cut** up to ~**150 mm**.
- ✅ **CNC accuracy** in modern shipyards: ±**0.1 mm** over a 20 m bed.
- ✅ **SAW (submerged arc welding)** follows OFC bevel preparation in the shipbuilding sequence.
- ✅ **Modern integrated lines** combine: **loading → shot blasting → marking → cutting → beveling → stacking** in a single transfer line.
- ✅ **ISO 9013** classifies thermal cut quality into **quality grades 1 to 4** based on perpendicularity, drag-line depth, and HAZ.
- ✅ **Water jet cutting (AWJ)** is the only method producing **no HAZ** for thick non-ferrous plate.
- ✅ **NC controller components**: DNC server, MMI (man-machine interface), PLC, servo drive, feedback encoder.
- ✅ **In photoelectric tracing**, white = high reflection (background), black = low reflection (line) — the head aligns to the boundary.
- ✅ **Y-bevel, K-bevel, X-bevel** all achievable in a single OFC pass with **3-torch bevel head** on CNC machines.
- ✅ **Plate utilization > 90%** is achieved by **nesting** + **common-line cutting** (sharing a cut edge between adjacent parts).

---

> **END OF MODULE 2 NOTES — CUTTING PROCESSES AND TOOLS**
